solution: Bepo succumbed to family pressure; his wife and daughters had already moved to the UK, and his hesitation was straining their relationship. This can be found on page 10 of the novel.

options.c: To reunite with his family and avoid marital collapse

question: What was unique about Bepo’s initial job interview at Stardom?
solution: The founder, Chief Aje, hired Bepo personally after a one-on-one discussion, unlike many others who faced the school's interview panels. This can be found on page 56 of the novel.

options.a: It was done over the phone
options.c: He was interviewed by the founder
options.d: He had to bribe the MD's father

question: What is the name of the director of Beesway Group of School?
solution: Mr Egi Meko is the founder of Beesway Group of School, the school that Mr Bepo worked in before he came to Stardom. He was the one who laughed off Bepo's correction of the phrase "Group of School" and refused to change the school's name. This can be found on page 34 of the novel.

question: What shocking behaviour did a UK student allegedly exhibit toward a Nigerian teacher, according to the novel?
solution: Bepo recalled the story as an example of culture shock for Nigerian teachers abroad. This can be found on page 13 of the novel where Bepo notes that he recalls reading the story of a Nigerian teacher who got settled the first day he walked into a class in a UK school and one of the students asked: "Are you an idiot?". Bepo, however, believes this is an isolated incident, if at all it is true, and should not deter him from relocating abroad to reunite with his family.

options.a: Insulting him with 'Are you an idiot?'

question: What gesture impressed Sola when her daughter Betty was hospitalised in the UK?
solution: The swift emergency response and compassionate care, including birthday gifts from doctors, touched her deeply. This can be found on page 17 of the novel.

options.b: Her husband was offered a job
options.c: Emergency care arrived in under 5 minutes
options.d: They were moved to a private hospital

question: What kind of job did Akindele, the older Nigerian migrant, do when he first arrived in the US?
solution: He worked first in freight loading, then in a correctional centre. This is one of the cautionary tales of migration that Bepo heard. It shows the constant grind in America by these immigrants. This can be found on page 18 of the novel.

question: What figure of speech is used in the expression "...wouldn't have to clean 'dishes', as folks uncharitably say about washing corpses."
solution: The phrase "clean dishes," when used figuratively in the context of washing corpses, could be interpreted as a euphemism. Euphemisms are a type of figurative language where a mild or indirect term is used to replace something that might be considered harsh, blunt or unpleasant. In this case, "clean dishes" softens or disguises the grim reality of washing corpses, making it sound more mundane or routine.

question: What city did Sola and her husband settle in within the UK?
solution: Sola and her husband settled in Manchester due to lower rent and cost of living. They could not afford the monthly rent of up to 1,500 or 1,000 pounds in London, so they had to stay in Manchester, where they rented a two-bedroom apartment for 650 pounds monthly.

question: What career did Bepo's wife Seri practice abroad?
solution: Seri, his wife, was already working as a nurse in the UK. This can be found on page 11 of the novel.

question: “Tears trickled out of his eyes… More tears streamed from both eyes, competitively…” is an example of—
solution: These words can be found on page 5 of the novel where they were used to describe Bepo's emotional state at the beginning of the text. Tears are personified as if they are racing or competing with each other.

question: “He brought his palms together like a supplicant” is an example of—
solution: This is used to describe how Mr Bepo tried to shield his teary face from the view of the students and staff. It compares his posture to that of someone begging or praying using “like”. This is the use of simile. The words can be found on page 5 of the novel.

question: The Yoruba proverb "The Oyingbo market never finds out a certain person did not even turn up" means
solution: The Yoruba proverb Oja Oyingbo ko mo' p'enikan o wa' ("The Oyingbo market never finds out a certain person did not even turn up") means that the market is "ever bustling" or always busy. The proverb can be found on page 60 of the novel. When Mr Bepo was said to have gone there to purchase some food items that his wife instructed him to bring along with him.

question: "If you beat my height, you can’t beat my eyes.” What was Bepo trying to communicate here?
solution: Bepo says this whenever he is invigilating an examination. Whenever he is present, according to him, no act of cheating will go uncaught. This can be found on page 19 of the novel.

options.a: Physical strength surpasses all other traits
options.b: No act of cheating will go uncaught
options.c: Height is the least important quality of a person
options.d: A competitive spirit always guarantee victory

question: Why does the narrator employ a conversational tone in the narration of Bepo's story?
solution: The friendly, conversational tone of the narrator in The Lekki Headmaster mirrors everyday Nigerian speech. This makes the story feel authentic, relatable and accessible to the reader. The reader can thus understand that the tale is a tale of everyday Nigerian life and that the subject matter applies to all of us.

options.a: To show multiple perspectives on the subject matter

question: When the director of Beesway references the Yoruba proverb about vegetables plucked from a dumpsite, what was he trying to communicate to Bepo?
solution: Mr Meko references the story of a hawker who plucked vegetables from a filthy dumpsite, and when a buyer calls to her, having no intention to probe the source of the product, immediately begins to assure the value of feigned quality. The hawker then retorts: "These vegetables are not from any dumpsite". With this proverb, Mr Meko is trying to hint at how people who feel guilty often defend what wasn't questioned.

options.b: People who feel guilty defend what wasn't questioned
options.c: People on the bottom can be destined to do greater things

question: What figure of speech is used in "He would look at the ceiling and at the faces before him, as though he had just returned from a dreamy wonderland”?
solution: This expression can be found on page 6 of the novel, where Bepo is in a state of emotional turmoil and has been taken to the VP's office. While they are trying to inquire what the reason for his emotional outburst is, he stared at the ceiling instead. His detachment from reality is compared to the detachment one gets when they have just returned from a dreamy wonderland.

question: “Mr. Audu, the Fine Arts teacher, who was a bunch of biting humour…” What does this description of Mr Audu mean?
solution: This expression can be found on page 6 of the novel where Mr Audu was described to be "a bunch of biting humour". He complained about how the MD was "a witch and wizard rolled into one" because of the way she was able to find a way to offset the cost from the Boarding House discount by increasing the Excursion fees. This is one of the examples that illustrate how Mr Audu is sarcastic and full of sharp, critical wit.

options.b: He is sarcastic and has sharp, critical wit
options.c: He is known for his apt innuendos

question: “...his foot-dragging over the matter had pushed his marriage to the brink.” What does "foot-dragging" mean as used in the novel?
solution: "Foot-dragging" means delay or unwillingness to take timely action on an issue. This expression can be found on page 10 of the novel. Where the reason for Bepo's emotional outburst is eventually revealed. The character's procrastination or hesitation had a detrimental effect on his marriage and he has no other option than to succumb to their demands of relocating to the UK.

question: Mrs. Ibidun Gloss expressed deep appreciation to Bepo for contributing what she called an "unrivalled quota" to the growth of Stardom. What does "unrivalled quota" as used in the novel mean?
solution: This expression can be found on page 56 of the novel where the MD was saying her farewell speech to Bepo, who was leaving stardom. She was trying to express her appreciation for the overwhelming and incomparable contribution that he has made to the growth of the school. The phrase "unrivalled quota" highlights that Bepo’s efforts were unmatched and outstanding, setting them apart from others. The word "unrivalled" emphasizes the uniqueness and superiority of Bepo's contribution, while "quota" refers to the portion or effort provided by Bepo to Stardom's growth.

options.a: Bepo's incomparable and exceptional contribution to the growth of the school
options.b: His savings over the years which is not comparable to the contributions of others
options.c: His impactful contribution to the development of the school's building
options.d: His buying of a large chunk of the school's shares

question: Which of the following is not a figure of speech used in J.P. Clark's poem "Ibadan" as referenced in the novel?
solution:
In J.P. Clark's "Ibadan":

Ibadan, running splash of rust

and gold-flung and scattered among

seven hills like broken china in the sun. 

The poem makes use of metaphor in the first line where the rustiness of the roofs is compared to a "running splash". Simile is used with the comparison of the rusted roofs to broken china in the sun. Imagery is also used because we can picture the descriptions given in our minds. However, onomatopoeia, the use of words that imitate or resemble the sound they describe, is not used in the poem.

question:
This question is based on Kabia Alabi Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

The following are current socio-economic realities depicted in the novel except
solution:
The novel addresses several socio-economic realities, including the Japa trend, which describes the mass emigration of Nigerians seeking better opportunities abroad. It highlights the challenges faced by bureaucracies, particularly when obtaining essential travel documents. Additionally, it portrays the struggles of middle-class professionals, such as teachers who are often undervalued and underpaid, as well as doctors and other health workers who choose to leave the country for "greener pastures" due to a lack of opportunities at home.

However, while the declining state of Nigerian universities is a significant socio-economic issue, it is not one of the themes explored in this novel.

options.a: The declining condition of Nigerian universities

question: One of the first things a child notices in birds is
solution: The passage explicitly states, "One of the first things that a child notices is how noisy many kinds of birds are".

question: What makes it easy to identify a species of birds in the wood?
solution: The passage explains that "many birds make characteristic calls, and with practice you can tell which species are in a wood just from these calls without seeing the birds at all".

question: Flocks of finches could easily become separated
solution: The passage states, "When flocks of finches are looking for insects or seeds on the branches of trees, they could easily become separated...".

options.a: when they get injured by other birds
options.b: when they get hungry after being separated from other birds
options.c: when they see predators hovering in the sky
options.d: when they hunt for insects or seeds on the branches of trees

question: The small birds have
solution:
The passage states that “at the sight of a bird of prey, a small bird will give its flying predator call as an alarm to other birds in the neighbourhood.”
This suggests that small birds use a particular type of call: "the flying predator call" to warn others of danger.

question: When a bird of prey is sighted, a small bird
solution: The text states that "at the sight of a bird of prey, a small bird will give its flying predator call as an alarm to other birds in the neighbourhood".

question: Fill the gap labelled 6
solution: The "United Nations Security Council" (UNSC) is the official and proper name of this specific UN organ.

question: Fill the gap labelled 7
solution: The standard phrase used in the UN Charter is "the maintenance of international peace and security."

question: Fill the gap labelled 8
solution: "Peace and security" is the fixed legal phrase for the Council's mandate.

question: Fill the gap labelled 9
solution: In this context, "members" refers to the individual seats/countries. "Members" (plural) is grammatically correct following the number fifteen.

question: Fill the gap labelled 10
solution: In international bodies, member states exercise their power by casting a vote on resolutions.

question: Fill in the gap labelled 11
solution: The founding document of the United Nations is called the UN Charter.

question: Fill in the gap labelled 12
solution: Under Article 39 of the Charter, the Council determines the existence of any "threat to the peace."

question: Fill in the gap labelled 13
solution: In legal and diplomatic terms, the groups involved in a conflict or dispute are referred to as the parties.

question: Fill the gap labelled 14
solution: A "settlement" is the formal term for the resolution or ending of a dispute or conflict.

question: Fill the gap labelled 15
solution: When diplomacy fails, the UN can impose economic or diplomatic sanctions to enforce its decisions without using military force.

question:
This question is based on Kabir Alabi Garba's The Lekki Headmaster

The school nurse, Mrs Titi, fetched a handkerchief and offered it to him because
solution:
This can be found on page 5 of the novel. The principal, Mr. Bepo Adewale, was weeping uncontrollably during the morning assembly. While several staff members, including the Vice Principal, gathered around to find out what was wrong, he continued to sob freely and could not utter a word.

Mrs. Titi (Nurse Titi) offered the handkerchief to him because he was a "weeper" in that moment of distress, and along with other staff, she began helping him back to his office as they were unable to get him to stop crying or explain his plight.

options.a: she always went about with an extra handkerchief
options.b: the Physics teacher, Pastor Ope Wande, had prayed over it
options.c: it was all she could do at the moment
options.d: she didn't have any drug immediately available

question:
This question is based on Kabir Alabi Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

"Red-tapism is found everywhere" means
solution:
In the novel, this concept is illustrated through Mr. Bepo’s experience while trying to renew his international passport:

The term refers to excessive bureaucracy or adherence to official rules and formalities that prevent or delay action.

Bepo faced numerous hurdles, including tales of new rules, hassles for applicants, and the non-availability of booklets. Although the government claimed the process could be done online without bribes, Bepo found that the online system was deliberately slow or erratic to frustrate applicants. Because of these excessive formalities, Bepo felt forced to use an "agent" to bypass the crowd and expedite the capturing process.

This whole ordeal can be found in chapter 10 of the novel.

options.b: Red tape worm is found everywhere

question:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster

'If you beat my height, you can't beat my eyes' is a statement by
solution: Bepo told students jovially that he was created to be a principal. He believed that because of his six-footer height and prominent eyeballs, no act of cheating could go uncaught. He would declare, "If you beat my height, you can't beat my eyes," whenever he was supervising an exam. This can be found on page 19 of the novel.

question:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

How did Sola and her husband travel to London from Manchester every day?
solution: Sola explained to Bepo that they lived in Manchester because they could not afford the high rents in London.She mentioned that travel by rail from Manchester to London was about 30 minutes. This can be found on page 16 of the novel

question:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster

"Yes....That is what you paid the extra amount for. If you had followed the normal channel, you would still be there with them."

To whom was this said?
solution: This was said to Bepo by a passport officer in Ibadan after he paid an extra amount to expedite his passport renewal. The officer confirmed that the extra payment allowed Bepo to bypass the large crowd following the "normal channel." This can be found on page 52 of the novel.

question:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

"So, you have taken your madness to this level?"

Where was the statement made?
solution: This statement was made by the director of Beesway Group of School, Mr. Egi Meko, to Bepo. Meko accused Bepo of plotting to destroy the school by discussing a grammatical error in the school's name with a parent during a PTA meeting. This can be found on page 35 of the novel.

question:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

Bepo considered establishing a school because
solution: Bepo would not mind establishing his own school because he had "garnered a lot of experience about teaching and school management" and it would give him an "opportunity to push his passion for moulding the young". This can be found on page 12 of the novel.

question:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

Bibi's scream diverted Mrs Ladele's attention from a movie on
solution: Mrs. Ladele was watching a movie where she was captivated by an "imminent bubble burst." Unknown to the doctor in the film, the doctor's husband was responsible for the pregnancy of the doctor's best friend. Mrs. Ladele was watching this painful truth unfold when she heard Bibi's scream. This can be found on page 22 of the novel.

question:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

Bepo's retirement plan suggests that he was
solution: Bepo began attending entrepreneurship seminars and planning for life after age 55, showing he was "thinking seriously" and had "pictured himself" in a position to employ others and pay taxes. This can be found on page 11 of the novel.

question:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

What did Bepo believe was crucial for success in the transportation business?
solution: Bepo believed that a major challenge in transportation was that many commercial drivers lacked enlightened business practices and were not trustworthy, which ruined the business dream. This can be found on page 12 of the novel.

question:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster. 

What was the essence of open days at Stardom Schools?
solution: For the management of Stardom Schools, Open Days served as an important opportunity to update parents on academic activities. It was a day intended for staff to have personal encounters with parents, which the school encouraged and sometimes compelled. While it was a time for parents to give gifts to specific staff , it was also a day when teachers expected to receive various complaints from parents. This can be found on page 27 of the novel.

options.c: to update parents on academic activities
options.d: to give teachers an opportunity to relax

question:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

One of the challenges faced in General Hospitals cited in the novel is
solution: The text notes that many people had reservations about government (general) hospitals. They dread them because of the large crowds they attracted. These hospitals were also described as being bogged down by inefficiency and corruption, often leaving doctors overwhelmed. This can be found on page 22 of the novel.

question:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster

The contestant for the position of a prefect must have spent ... in the school.
solution: To be an aspirant for a prefect position at Stardom Schools, the student must have been in the school for up to two sessions. Additionally, the contestant was expected to have a good moral history, be above average academically and must not owe any school fees. This can be found on page 41 of the novel.

question:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

Bibi is always scared of Mr Ayesoro because of his
solution: Bibi was scared of Mr. Ayesoro because of the deep tribal marks he flaunted on his face. Because of these prominent incisions, students nicknamed him "Mr. Owala," a derogatory term for a person with wild facial marks. This can be found on page 22 of the novel.

question:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

The stories of Nigerian migrants such as Sola, Hope, Riike and Akindele showed that, for thos who have 'ja pa'
solution: The collective stories of these migrants demonstrated that moving abroad (the "ja pa" phenomenon) involves significant struggles. Hope was abandoned by his wife and lost his sponsorship. Akindele described the exhaustion of working "for every dime" and every "cent," noting there is no easy job in America. Sola lived in cramped, non-furnished housing and changed jobs frequently just to survive. These tales can be found in chapter 3 of the novel.

options.a: it is the final goodbye to Nigeria
options.b: those still in Nigeria are wasting their time
options.c: life is not a bed of roses
options.d: there is no cause for alarm

question:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

Why was the Government teacher named 'Owala'?
solution: "Owala" is a derogatory Yoruba appellation for a person with wild facial marks. Mr. Ayesoro was popular for the deep tribal marks he flaunted on his face. This can be found on page 22 of the novel.

question:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster

How did Stardom Schools curb late-coming?
solution: Stardom Schools managed to greatly reduce lateness by lowering its boarding fees from N250,000 to N165,000 per session. This incentive led over 80 percent of parents to move their children to the boarding house, resulting in almost all students arriving by 7:45am for assembly. This can be found on page 6 of the novel.

options.b: By publishing names of latecomers in the school magazine

question:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

Bepo worked in Stardom for over
solution: Mr. Bepo had been an inspiring pillar at Stardom for 24 years (which is over two decades). This can be found on page 56 of the novel.

question:
The question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

What joke did Bepo tell his students concerning his big eyes?
solution: Bepo jovially told his students that his big eyes will always catch whoever was cheating. He claimed he was "created to be a principal" and would tell students, "If you beat my height, you can't beat my eyes" during exams. This can be found on page 19 of the novel.

options.a: his big eyes will always catch whoever was cheating
options.b: he said the eyes were always open even when he slept
options.c: Bepo said he was also an eye-master
options.d: he said his mum ate an owl when she carried his pregnancy

question:
This question is based on Kabir Garba's The Lekki Headmaster.

"It's like hanging a snake in the roof and going to bed". Who made this statement in The Lekki Headmaster?
solution: The statement was made by Chief Mrs. Solape Bayo, the chairman of the board of directors (and the MD's mother). She was expressing fear that the staff might use the large sum of money in their cooperative society to "rebel" and establish their own rival school. This can be found on page 26 of the novel.

question:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

No sooner had Tobi left the house _____ it began to rain.
solution:
In English grammar, certain phrases are known as correlative conjunctions. They are pairs of words that always go together.

  • No sooner... than: This is the standard pair. Because "sooner" is a comparative adjective (ending in -er), it must be followed by "than," just like "faster than" or "taller than."

  • Hardly/Scarcely... when: This is a different structure often confused with the one above. If the sentence had started with "Hardly had Tobi left...," then "when" would be the correct choice.

question:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

What goes around _____ around.
solution: This is a common English proverb, and its wording is fixed. Proverbs usually follow a specific structure that doesn't change based on conversational context. The first part of the sentence uses the simple present tense (goes). The second part should also use the simple present tense (comes).

question:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

The boy _____ the flute for about thirty minutes.
solution: This tense is used for an action that started in the past and is still continuing into the present. The phrase "for about thirty minutes" indicates this. The structure for this tense is has/have + been + verb-ing.

question:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

Each of the answers ______ correct.
solution: The word "Each" is the actual subject of the sentence, not "answers". In English, "Each" is always treated as a singular pronoun. Even though "answers" is plural, the verb must agree with "Each."

question:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

Each of the reports ______ been carefully reviewed by the committee
solution:
The subject is "Each". Even though "reports" is plural, the verb must agree with the singular "Each".

The sentence structure requires an auxiliary verb to pair with "been" to form the present perfect tense. Has is the singular form

question:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

The ____ of armed robbery has been on the increase.
solution: Incidence refers to the rate or frequency of something happening (e.g., crime rates). Incident refers to a single, specific event. Because the sentence mentions being "on the increase," it is talking about the frequency/rate of the crime, so "incidence" is the correct choice.

question:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

The doctor ____ him to take his medicine regularly.
solution: Advise is the verb. It is the action of giving a recommendation. Advice, the noun, is the recommendation itself.  Since the doctor is performing an action in the past, the correct past-tense verb is "advised."

question:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

He was praised for the ______ achievement.
solution: "Laudable" is an adjective meaning "deserving praise and commendation".

question:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

It is high time you _____ visiting that friend of yours.
solution:
When you use the phrases "It is time," "It is high time" or "It is about time" followed by a subject (in this case, "you"), you must use the Past Simple tense of the verb.

Even though the sentence refers to the present or future, we use the past tense to indicate that the action is overdue or should have happened already. This is known as the unreal past or the subjunctive mood.

question:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

Put Chioma's meat in _____ pack.
solution:
The standard hierarchy for adjectives in English is often remembered by the acronym OSASCOMP:

Order Category Examples from the Question
1 Opinion Shiny
2 Size (Not present)
3 Age New
4 Shape (Not present)
5 Color Red
6 Origin (Not present)
7 Material (Not present)
8 Purpose Lunch (describing the type of pack)

Putting them together gives you: "shiny new red lunch pack."

question:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

As trained teachers, Joke teaches _______ better than Jumoke.
solution: The word "better" is a comparative adjective. When we want to emphasise or strengthen a comparative, we use specific intensifiers. "Far" is a standard intensifier used with comparative forms (e.g., far better, far taller, far more expensive). "Much" is another common choice (much better).

question:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

The teacher asked the students to submit their assignments before the end of ____ week
solution: In this sentence, the context implies a deadline for a future action. "The teacher asked the students to submit..." indicates an instruction for something that needs to happen after the request was made. "Before the end of next week" is a standard way to set a future deadline.

question:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

If I had worked hard, I _______ very high marks in the exam.
solution: This is a Third Conditional sentence, used to talk about a past situation that didn't happen and its imaginary result. The structure is: If + Past Perfect (had worked), ... would have + Past Participle (scored).

question:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

It had been raining before the party started, _____
solution:
Question tags follow a specific rule: they must use the same auxiliary verb used in the main sentence, but in the opposite form (positive sentence = negative tag).

  • Main Verb: "had been" (Positive)

  • Tag: "hadn't" (Negative) + "it" (Subject)

question:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

That Fulani man has a large _____ of sheep.
solution:
In English, specific groups of animals have unique collective nouns.

  • Flock: The correct term for a group of sheep or birds.

  • Herd: used for cattle, elephants, or goats.

  • Swine: An old-fashioned term for pigs, not a collective noun.

question:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

She is ______ a sailor who is away at sea.
solution: The sentence suggests emotional desire or yearning. A sailor who is away at sea is missed, not just awaited.

question:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

Scarcely ______ she entered the room when the phone rang.
solution:
When a sentence begins with a negative or restrictive word like Scarcely, Hardly, or No sooner, the subject and verb must be inverted.

Structure: Scarcely + had + subject + past participle (entered).

question:
In each of the following questions, choose the option that best completes the gap(s).

He presented a masterpiece at the conference, ___________
solution:
For sentences in the Past Simple without an auxiliary verb (like "presented"), we use "did/didn't" in the tag.

  • Main Verb: "presented" (Positive Past Simple)

  • Tag: "didn't" (Negative) + "he" (Subject)

question:
For this question, choose the option nearest in meaning to the word or phrase in italics.

The students were reluctant to participate in the debate.
solution: Reluctant means feeling or showing hesitation or a lack of desire to do something. Unwilling is a direct synonym, meaning not ready, eager or prepared to do something.

question:
For this question, choose the option nearest in meaning to the word or phrase in italics.

Parenting is really arduous even though enjoyable.
solution: Arduous describes a task that is difficult and requires a great deal of effort or energy. Strenuous is the closest synonym, as it also means requiring or using great exertion or effort.

question:
For this question, choose the option nearest in meaning to the word or phrase in italics.

The debater made a very scurrilous remark about his opponent.
solution: Scurrilous refers to making or spreading scandalous claims about someone with the intention of damaging their reputation; it is characterised by coarseness or vulgarity. Abusive is the closest match as it implies harsh, insulting, or vitriolic language.

question:
For this question, choose the option nearest in meaning to the word or phrase in italics.

Giwa does odious things at times.
solution: Odious means extremely unpleasant, repulsive, or deserving of hatred. Horrible is a close synonym for something that is very unpleasant or shocking.

question:
For this question, choose the option nearest in meaning to the word or phrase in italics.

Gambling is a pernicious act.
solution: Pernicious describes something that has a harmful effect, especially in a gradual or subtle way. Noxious means harmful, poisonous, or very unpleasant. It is the closest synonym among the choices provided because it also denotes something damaging or destructive.

question:
For this question, choose the option nearest in meaning to the word or phrase in italics.

The building was haunted by a couple of spooks.
solution: Spooks is an informal term for ghosts or spirits. Ghosts are the soul or spirit of a dead person that appears to the living, which is the direct synonym for "spooks" in this context.

question:
For this question, choose the option nearest in meaning to the word or phrase in italics.

The scientist made an astonishing discovery in the laboratory.
solution: Scientific discoveries are described by their impact or the level of surprise they cause. "Astonishing" means extremely surprising or impressive; "amazing" is a direct synonym.

question:
For this question, choose the option nearest in meaning to the word or phrase in italics.

Between Achebe and Soyinka, it is debatable who is more enigmatic a writer.
solution: "Enigmatic" refers to someone or something that is mysterious, puzzling or difficult to understand. "Inscrutable" means impossible to understand or interpret.

question:
For this question, choose the option nearest in meaning to the word or phrase in italics.

When the landlady noticed the silhouette on the wall, she called up her son.
solution: A "silhouette" is the dark shape and outline of someone or something visible against a lighter background

question:
For this question, choose the option opposite in meaning to the word or phrase in italics. 

Musa vehemently opposed his son's political ambition.
solution: "Vehemently" means doing something with great force, passion or intense feeling. While "vehemently" implies heat and passion, "coldly" implies a lack of emotion, passion or intensity, making it the most appropriate antonym in this context.

question:
For this question, choose the option opposite in meaning to the word or phrase in italics. 

The natives were allowed to stay.
solution: "Natives" refers to people born in a specified place or associated with a place by birth. The opposite is "foreigners," which refers to people who come from a different country or place.

question:
For this question, choose the option opposite in meaning to the word or phrase in italics. 

The scientist conducted a meticulous experiment.
solution: Meticulous" means showing great attention to detail and being very careful. The direct opposite is "careless," which implies a lack of attention or thought.

question:
For this question, choose the option opposite in meaning to the word or phrase in italics. 

In the past, anyone who angered the gods would be killed.
solution: To "anger" someone is to make them feel strong displeasure or hostility. The most direct opposite is to "please" someone, meaning to give them pleasure or satisfaction. However, in a context that relates to the gods, "appeased" is the better answer. Action is taken (like a sacrifice or prayer) to specifically remove that anger and bring the gods back to a neutral or favourable state.

question:
For this question, choose the option opposite in meaning to the word or phrase in italics. 

Despite their efforts at the last Nations Cup fiesta, the team was chastised by their employers.
solution: "Chastised" means to rebuke or punish severely. To be "honoured" is the opposite, as it involves giving someone public respect or an award for their efforts.

question:
For this question, choose the option opposite in meaning to the word or phrase in italics. 

He made a facile generalisation.
solution: "Facile" describes an argument or theory that is too simple and ignores the true complexities of an issue. Therefore, "complex" is the appropriate opposite.

question:
For this question, choose the option opposite in meaning to the word or phrase in italics. 

The manager became flexible on critical matters.
solution: "Flexible" means ready and able to change or adapt. "Rigid" means unable to bend or be forced out of shape.

question:
For this question, choose the option opposite in meaning to the word or phrase in italics. 

The gear is immanent in a car.
solution: "Immanent" refers to something existing or operating within; "transcendent" refers to something that exists beyond or above a physical limit.

question:
For this question, choose the option opposite in meaning to the word or phrase in italics. 

Andrew is the protagonist in the play.
solution: The "protagonist" is the leading character. The "antagonist" is the character who actively opposes the protagonist.

question:
For this question, choose the option opposite in meaning to the word or phrase in italics. 

The robbers gained access to the bank because of their porous security network.
solution: "Porous" means having spaces or holes through which liquid or air may pass (metaphorically, security gaps). "Impermeable" means not allowing fluid to pass through.

question:
For this question, select the option that best explains the information conveyed in the sentence. 

Tom's excuses don't hold water.
solution: If an argument or theory "holds water," it means it is valid, logical or can stand up to scrutiny.  When we say excuses "don't hold water," we mean they are inconsistent or unbelievable. They "leak" because they aren't solid enough to be true.

options.a: The excuses are not logically sound
options.b: The excuses are acceptable by a few
options.d: The excuses explain the situation well

question:
For this question, select the option that best explains the information conveyed in the sentence. 

He got a job by paying hush money.
solution: The sentence uses the idiom "hush money," which refers to money paid to someone to prevent them from revealing potentially damaging or illegal information. It means that he bribed to get the job.

options.a: He bribed to get the job
options.b: He was induced to get the job
options.c: He paid exorbitant fee to get the job
options.d: He was paid for the job

question:
For this question, select the option that best explains the information conveyed in the sentence. 

The chairman of the association tells his members not to throw in the towel.
solution: To "throw in the towel" is a common idiom meaning to admit defeat or to stop trying to do something because you know you cannot succeed. This phrase comes from boxing; when a fighter's coach sees their athlete is too injured or exhausted to continue, they literally throw a towel into the ring to signal a forfeit.

options.b: They are not to give up
options.c: They should be ready for a trip

question:
For this question, select the option that best explains the information conveyed in the sentence. 

Despite his wealth, Mr Oluboye is known for tightening his belt.
solution: To "tighten one's belt" is an idiom that means to live more frugally or spend less money than usual

options.b: He is very careful with his spending
options.c: He refuses to wear a belt at all

question:
For this question, select the option that best explains the information conveyed in the sentence. 

The students felt the lecture was a load of crap.
solution: "A load of crap" is a vulgar slang idiom used to describe something that is perceived as nonsense or of very poor quality.

options.a: The students believe the lecture was too long
options.b: The students refused to listen to the lecturer
options.c: The students thought the lecture was stupid
options.d: The students did not attend the lecture

question:
For this question, select the option that best explains the information conveyed in the sentence. 

The boy accused his friend of putting a spoke in his wheel.
solution: This idiom means to deliberately prevent someone from carrying out a plan or to hinder their progress. In the question, the boy is accusing his friend of sabotaging or blocking his efforts.

options.a: He accused his friend of making jest of him
options.b: He accused his friend of spoiling his bicycle wheel
options.c: He accused his friend of destroying his move of getting a new wheel
options.d: He accused his friend of thwarting the execution of his plan

question:
For this question, select the option that best explains the information conveyed in the sentence. 

My boss is such a slave driver, I had to work two weekends in a row.
solution: A "slave driver" is a person who makes others work very hard in an unkind or demanding way. The speaker mentions having to "work two weekends in a row," which illustrates being overworked by their superior.

options.a: He is a business owner who is very rich
options.b: He is a leader who is considerate
options.c: He is a taskmaster who overworks employees
options.d: He is a manager who is friendly

question:
For this question, select the option that best explains the information conveyed in the sentence. 

"Cinema, tonight?" he asked. "Maybe another time", she replied.
solution: "Cinema, tonight?" is a casual way of suggesting or inviting someone to go to the cinema. "Maybe another time" is used to politely decline an invitation for the present while suggesting the possibility of accepting it in the future.

options.a: He asked her if she will go to the cinema and she vehemently declined it
options.b: He suggested taking her to a cinema tonight and she said probably another time
options.c: He suggested going to the cinema with her and she agreed
options.d: He asked to take her to a cinema tonight and she accepted

question:
For this question, select the option that best explains the information conveyed in the sentence. 

Peter is a man of letters.
solution: A "man of letters" refers to a man who is a scholar or a writer, particularly one who is well-versed in literature and the humanities.

options.b: He likes to read a lot
options.d: He is a great literary artist

question:
For this question, the words in capital letters have emphatic stress. Choose the option to which the given sentence relates.

They came with me to the MEETING.
solution: In emphatic stress questions, the sentence provided is the answer to a specific question. The stressed word (MEETING) is the "new" or "correcting" information.  To emphasise "MEETING," the speaker must be correcting a different location mentioned in the question. Therefore, the question must have suggested a different place, like a party.

options.a: Did they come to a meeting with me?
options.b: Did they arrive with me to the meeting?
options.c: Did they come with me to the party?
options.d: Who came with me to the meeting?

question:
For this question, the word in capital letters has the emphatic stress. Choose the option to which the sentence relates.

The woman was delivered of a baby BOY.
solution: Since "BOY" is stressed, the speaker is correcting the gender. This would be the response if someone had mistakenly asked if it was a girl.

options.a: Was the girl delivered of a baby girl?
options.b: Was the woman delivered of two baby boys?
options.c: Was the woman delivered of a baby girl?
options.d: Was the girl delivered of a baby boy?

question:
For this question, the word in capital letter has the emphatic stress. Choose the option to which the given sentence relates.

A HAWKER showed him the way to the park.
solution: Since "HAWKER" is stressed, the speaker is emphasising who did the showing. This corrects an alternative, such as a trader.

options.a: Did a hawker show him the way to the street?
options.b: Did a trader show him the way to the park?
options.c: Did the hawker show her the way to the park?
options.d: Did the hawker show him the way to the park?

question:
In the following questions, the word in capital letters has the emphatic stress. Choose the option to which the given word relates. 

The doctor examined the patient with a STETHOSCOPE.
solution: Since STETHOSCOPE is stressed, the speaker is correcting the instrument used. The question must suggest a different tool, like a microscope.

options.a: Did the doctor examine the matron with a stethoscope?
options.b: Did the nurse examine the patient with a stethoscope?
options.c: Did the doctor examine the patient with a microscope?
options.d: Did the doctor cure the patient with a stethoscope?
